refs.note: The pontifical hearing of late September 57 BC at which the twenty-strong college freed Cicero's Palatine site is the same hearing whose proceedings are the body of De Domo Sua. The list of attendees here is the more complete record.

The ``mind you had lost, hand with which you had ruined it'' passage echoes the De Domo Sua attack on Clodius's consecration of the Palatine site (with the famous Tanagran courtesan-statue at Dom. 111-112).
The Bona Dea outrage of December 62 BC --- the singed-by-religious-fires figure of section 4 --- returns at section 37 as the climactic prosecution-of-Clodius head of the haruspical text.
The catalogue of Clodius's Bona Dea costume (saffron robe, headband, slippers, breast-band, lyre) is the most concentrated form of the figure used variously across the post-exile speeches.

Pompey's leadership of the Senate-front against Clodius's transitio ad plebem the year before (61 BC) is described here from Cicero's senatorial perspective; the Atticus letters of 61-60 BC give the unfiltered version of the same.
The dagger in the temple of Castor and the home-confinement of Pompey under Clodius's tribunate is the same set of incidents covered at the opening of Post Reditum in Senatu.

The bill against the lex Aelia and lex Fufia (gutting the religious-obstructionist machinery) was a central charge in the cross-examination of Vatinius and recurs here in the catalogue of Clodian wickedness against the country.
